channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: TraceSafe: A Systematic Assessment of LLM Guardrails on Multi-Step Tool-Calling Trajectories
item.description: As LLMs evolve into autonomous agents performing multi-step tool calls, safety vulnerabilities shift from final outputs to intermediate execution traces—yet no benchmark systematically assesses *mid-trajectory* risks. We introduce **TraceSafe-Bench**, the first comprehensive benchmark covering 12 risk categories (e.g., prompt injection, privacy leaks, hallucinations, interface inconsistencies) across 1,000+ realistic, structured tool-call trajectories. Evaluating 13 LLM-as-guard models and 7 specialized guardrails, we find: (1) Structural data competence (e.g., JSON parsing) dominates semantic safety alignment—performance correlates strongly with structured reasoning ($ρ = 0.79$) but negligibly with jailbreak robustness ($ρ ≈ 0.03$); (2) General-purpose LLM architectures outperform specialized safety models consistently, indicating architecture matters more than scale or domain tuning; (3) Detection accuracy improves over longer trajectories as models leverage dynamic behavioral patterns—not static tool definitions—boosting late-stage recall by up to 12.4%. These results underscore that securing agentic workflows requires joint optimization of structural reasoning and safety alignment.

item.title: SentinelSphere: Integrating AI-Powered Real-Time Threat Detection with Cybersecurity Awareness Training
item.description: SentinelSphere is an AI-native platform that unifies real-time threat detection with adaptive cybersecurity awareness training to address the dual crisis of global talent shortage and persistent human-factor vulnerabilities. Its detection module employs an Enhanced Deep Neural Network trained on CIC-IDS2017/CIC-DDoS2019, augmented with novel HTTP-layer feature engineering for precise application-level attack identification. The education module leverages a quantized, domain-fine-tuned Phi-4 (Q4_K_M) LLM—deployable on commodity hardware (16 GB RAM, no GPU)—to deliver conversational, scenario-based security coaching. Experiments show the Enhanced DNN achieves 99.2% accuracy, reduces false positives by 37.5% versus baselines, and maintains >98.6% recall across DDoS, brute-force, and web exploits. Validation workshops confirmed high usability of its Traffic Light visualization and AI assistant among non-technical users. SentinelSphere demonstrates that tightly integrating intelligent detection with LLM-driven education meaningfully bridges technical and human security gaps in a single, deployable framework.

item.title: SkillTrojan: Backdoor Attacks on Skill-Based Agent Systems
item.description: Skill-based agent systems improve modularity and scalability by composing reusable skills—but introduce a critical, underexplored security surface: the skill implementations themselves. We propose **SkillTrojan**, the first backdoor attack that targets *skills as code*, not model weights or training data. It embeds malicious logic inside semantically plausible skills, partitions an encrypted payload across multiple benign invocations, and reconstructs/executes it only upon a predefined trigger. Crucially, SkillTrojan supports automated synthesis of backdoored skills from arbitrary templates, enabling scalable, stealthy propagation. To enable rigorous evaluation, we release a dataset of 3,000+ curated backdoored skills covering diverse patterns and configurations. Evaluated on EHR SQL with GPT-5.2-1211-Global, SkillTrojan achieves up to **97.2% attack success rate (ASR)** while preserving **89.3% clean-task accuracy (ACC)**—demonstrating high stealth and efficacy. This exposes a fundamental blind spot in current skill-based architectures and calls for defenses that explicitly reason about skill composition, execution flow, and contextual triggers.

item.title: FedDAP: Domain-Aware Prototype Learning for Federated Learning under Domain Shift
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) suffers from severe performance degradation under domain shift, as clients hold data from heterogeneous domains. While prototype-based FL methods improve robustness, they ignore domain structure by aggregating all local prototypes into a single domain-agnostic global prototype and enforcing uniform feature alignment across domains. To address this, we propose **FedDAP**, the first FL framework that constructs **domain-specific global prototypes** via similarity-weighted fusion of local prototypes *within the same domain*. During local training, clients perform **dual alignment**: (i) pulling features toward prototypes of their *own* domain, and (ii) pushing features away from prototypes of *other* domains. This enhances domain-aware representation learning while preserving cross-domain generalizability. Extensive experiments on DomainNet, Office-10, and PACS show FedDAP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods by +3.2–5.8% average accuracy under domain shift, with ablation confirming the critical role of domain-aware prototype fusion and dual alignment. Code is publicly available.

item.title: FlowExtract: Procedural Knowledge Extraction from Maintenance Flowcharts
item.description: Maintenance procedures in manufacturing are frequently documented as static flowcharts (PDFs/scans), encoding critical procedural knowledge—but remaining inaccessible to digital operator support systems. Vision-language models (VLMs) fail to reliably reconstruct directed connectivity topology from such ISO 5807-compliant diagrams due to arrow ambiguity, line crossings, and domain-specific layout complexity. FlowExtract addresses this by decoupling node detection (using YOLOv8 + EasyOCR for robust symbol and text extraction) from edge reconstruction. Its novel edge detection method identifies arrowheads geometrically and traces connecting lines *backward* to source nodes—significantly improving precision over VLM-based approaches. Evaluated on industrial troubleshooting guides, FlowExtract achieves 98.2% node F1 and 89.6% edge accuracy, outperforming state-of-the-art VLM baselines by +42.3 points on edge extraction. The open-source implementation enables practical, scalable conversion of legacy flowcharts into queryable procedural knowledge graphs.

item.title: A Parameter-Efficient Transfer Learning Approach through Multitask Prompt Distillation and Decomposition for Clinical NLP
item.description: We propose **MT-PDD**, a parameter-efficient multitask prompt distillation and decomposition framework for clinical NLP. It learns a single shared *metaprompt* from 21 diverse clinical source tasks and adapts it to unseen target tasks with **<0.05% trainable parameters**, eliminating per-task prompt duplication. Evaluated across 5 clinical NLP task types on 10 held-out datasets using LLaMA 3.1 8B, Meditron3 8B, and gpt-oss 20B, MT-PDD consistently outperforms LoRA by **1.5–1.7% absolute gain** despite using orders-of-magnitude fewer parameters, and surpasses single-task prompt tuning by **6.1–6.6%**. The gpt-oss 20B variant achieves state-of-the-art performance—especially on clinical reasoning tasks—demonstrating superior transferability of the distilled metaprompt representation in zero- and few-shot settings.

item.title: SubFLOT: Submodel Extraction for Efficient and Personalized Federated Learning via Optimal Transport
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative training but suffers from system and statistical heterogeneity, hindering real-world deployment. While federated pruning alleviates resource constraints, existing approaches face a critical trade-off: server-side pruning lacks personalization, whereas client-side pruning is computationally prohibitive for edge devices—and both exacerbate parametric divergence among heterogeneous submodels, impairing convergence. To address this, we propose **SubFLOT**, a novel server-side framework for *personalized* federated pruning. SubFLOT introduces two key innovations: (**i**) an **Optimal Transport-enhanced Pruning (OTP)** module that treats historical client models as proxies for local data distributions, formulating submodel extraction as a Wasserstein distance minimization to generate data-free, customized submodels; and (**ii**) a **Scaling-based Adaptive Regularization (SAR)** module that penalizes submodel deviation from the global model with strength scaled by the client’s pruning rate—effectively curbing divergence. Extensive experiments across six heterogeneous benchmarks show SubFLOT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving up to 7.9% higher accuracy, 68% less communication cost, and significantly improved personalization and convergence stability.

item.title: The Detection--Extraction Gap: Models Know the Answer Before They Can Say It
item.description: This paper identifies the **detection–extraction gap**: modern reasoning models internally “know” the correct answer long before standard decoding can reliably extract it. Across five model configurations (including Llama-3, Qwen, and Phi-3), two families, and three benchmarks (GSM8K, MMLU-Math, AQuA), we find that **52–88% of chain-of-thought tokens are generated after the answer is already recoverable from a partial prefix**. Free continuations from prefixes as short as 10% of the full trace recover the correct answer in most cases, whereas forced extraction (e.g., via “Answer:” prompts) fails on 42% of those same instances—revealing a fundamental mismatch between model state and prompt-conditioned decoding. We formalize this via a total-variation bound quantifying suffix-induced distributional shift. Leveraging this asymmetry, we propose **Black-box Adaptive Early Exit (\BAEE{})**, which uses free continuations for *both* detection and extraction—requiring no model modification or training. \BAEE{} reduces serial generation by **70–78%**, improves accuracy by **1–5 percentage points** across all models, and achieves up to **5.8 pp gain** for thinking-mode models; its cost-optimized variant cuts computation by **68–73%** at a median of just **9 API calls**. Code: https://github.com/EdWangLoDaSc/know2say.

item.title: LLM-based Schema-Guided Extraction and Validation of Missing-Person Intelligence from Heterogeneous Data Sources
item.description: This paper introduces the **Guardian Parser Pack**, a schema-first, auditable AI pipeline for extracting and normalizing missing-person intelligence from heterogeneous sources (e.g., forms, posters, web profiles). It integrates multi-engine PDF/OCR extraction, rule-based source identification, schema-guided harmonization, and—optionally—an LLM-assisted pathway with validator-guided repair and shared geocoding. Evaluated on 75 gold-aligned cases and 517 records, the LLM pathway achieved substantially higher extraction quality (F1 = 0.8664 vs. 0.2578) and key-field completeness (96.97% vs. 93.23%) than the deterministic baseline. Crucially, all LLM outputs passed initial schema validation—demonstrating built-in robustness—while runtime remained 130× slower (3.95 s vs. 0.03 s/record). Results validate a controlled, schema-anchored deployment of probabilistic AI in high-stakes investigative workflows.

item.title: SkillSieve: A Hierarchical Triage Framework for Detecting Malicious AI Agent Skills
item.description: SkillSieve is a hierarchical triage framework designed to detect malicious skills in AI agent marketplaces like ClawHub—where 13–26% of 13,000+ community skills harbor security vulnerabilities. It overcomes the modality gap between code (e.g., AST/regex) and natural-language instructions (e.g., `SKILL.md`) by applying three progressive analysis layers: Layer 1 uses lightweight, zero-cost XGBoost-scoring of regex, AST, and metadata to filter ~86% of benign skills in <40ms; Layer 2 delegates suspicious skills to an LLM via four parallel, structured sub-tasks (intent alignment, permission justification, covert behavior detection, cross-file consistency); Layer 3 resolves high-risk cases via a voting-and-debate jury of three diverse LLMs. Evaluated on 49,592 real skills and adversarial samples across five evasion techniques—fully deployed on a 440 ARM SBC—SkillSieve achieves **0.800 F1** on a 400-sample labeled benchmark (vs. ClawVet’s 0.421) at **$0.006/skill** average cost. Code, data, and benchmark are open-sourced.

item.title: Swiss-Bench 003: Evaluating LLM Reliability and Adversarial Security for Swiss Regulatory Contexts
item.description: Swiss-Bench 003 (SBP-003) introduces the first empirically grounded, dual-axis evaluation framework for LLMs in Swiss regulatory contexts—jointly assessing *production reliability* and *adversarial security*. Extending HAAS to eight dimensions, it adds D7 (self-graded reliability proxy) and D8 (adversarial security), and evaluates ten frontier models on 808 Swiss-specific, multilingual (DE/FR/IT/EN) items across seven newly developed benchmarks aligned with FINMA Guidance 08/2024, the revised nDSG, and OWASP LLM Top 10. Key findings: D7 self-scores (73–94%) vastly exceed D8 security scores (20–61%), revealing a critical confidence-robustness gap; prompt leakage resistance varies widely (24.8–88.2%), while PII extraction defense remains uniformly weak (14–42%); Qwen 3.5 Plus achieves top D7 (94.4%), and cost-efficient GPT-oss 120B attains highest D8 (60.7%). All evaluations are zero-shot under default provider settings; D7 is model-self-assessed and not independently validated. Conceptual mapping tables explicitly link benchmark dimensions to regulatory requirements for audit-ready compliance.

item.title: From Incomplete Architecture to Quantified Risk: Multimodal LLM-Driven Security Assessment for Cyber-Physical Systems
item.description: Cyber-physical systems (CPS) frequently suffer from incomplete or outdated architectural documentation, hindering reliable security assessment. To address this, we propose **ASTRAL**, an architecture-centric security assessment framework powered by multimodal LLMs. ASTRAL reconstructs system architectures from fragmented data sources (e.g., logs, diagrams, configs) via prompt chaining and few-shot learning, then integrates LLM-based reasoning with architectural modeling to identify threats and quantify risk—yielding interpretable, confidence-weighted scores for exposure, cascade probability, and recovery effort. Evaluated across three real-world CPS case studies and validated by 14 expert practitioners, ASTRAL achieves +38.2% higher architecture element recall and +51.7% broader threat coverage versus baseline methods, with strong inter-rater agreement (κ = 0.82). Practitioners rated it highly usable for architecture review and risk-informed decision-making.

item.title: Analogical Reasoning as a Doctor: A Foundation Model for Gastrointestinal Endoscopy Diagnosis
item.description: We introduce RATNet, a foundation model for gastrointestinal endoscopy diagnosis grounded in *analogical reasoning*—a novel paradigm that matches image-derived posterior knowledge to a learned prior knowledge base and transfers *relative relational knowledge* (e.g., “lesion A differs from B similarly to how C differs from D”) rather than relying on absolute label correspondence. Trained via cyclic pre-training across five heterogeneous endoscopy datasets without manual annotation alignment, RATNet features an encoder–RAT module–projector–multi-task head architecture, supporting fine-tuning, linear probing, and zero-shot transfer. In comprehensive evaluations across six clinically critical scenarios—including common disease diagnosis, few-shot rare disease recognition, zero-shot cross-site deployment, long-tailed robustness, novel disease adaptation, and privacy-preserving federated learning—RATNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art foundation models (e.g., GastroNet, GastroVision). Its analogical mechanism enhances generalization, mitigates annotation bias, reduces data acquisition costs, and enables practical deployment in resource-constrained settings. Code and models are open-sourced.

item.title: From Large Language Model Predicates to Logic Tensor Networks: Neurosymbolic Offer Validation in Regulated Procurement
item.description: We propose a neurosymbolic pipeline for auditable offer validation in regulated public procurement, integrating large language models (LLMs) with Logic Tensor Networks (LTNs). First, an LLM acts as a semantic predicate extractor—deriving interpretable, probabilistic truth values (e.g., “*bid amount ≤ ceiling*”) directly from unstructured offer documents. Second, these predicates are grounded in an LTN to perform differentiable first-order logical reasoning under domain-specific regulatory rules (e.g., “*if valid_license ∧ compliant_price then recommend_award*”). Evaluated on a real-world corpus of 1,247 procurement offers and 38 compliance clauses, our approach achieves competitive performance (F1 = 0.892 vs. 0.901 for SOTA black-box models), while uniquely enabling full decision traceability: every output is justified by concrete text spans, predicate scores, and activated logical rules. This provides native XAI support, modular predicate maintenance, and legally verifiable audit trails—addressing critical trust and compliance requirements in high-stakes public sector AI deployment.

item.title: Auditable Agents
item.description: This paper introduces *Auditable Agents*, arguing that accountability for LLM-based agents is impossible without built-in auditability—the system property enabling post-hoc reconstruction, compliance verification, and responsibility attribution. We formalize auditability along five dimensions: action recoverability, lifecycle coverage, policy checkability, responsibility attribution, and evidence integrity. To realize these, we identify three complementary mechanism classes—*detect*, *enforce*, and *recover*—whose temporal constraints explain why no single technique suffices. Supporting evidence is layered: ecosystem analysis reveals 617 security flaws across six open-source agent projects, indicating widespread failure to meet basic auditability prerequisites; runtime evaluation shows tamper-evident pre-execution mediation adds only 8.3 ms median overhead; and controlled recovery experiments demonstrate partial responsibility-relevant information can be reconstructed even when conventional logs are absent. We propose the *Auditability Card* as a standardized assessment tool and outline six open research problems, categorized by mechanism class.

item.title: LanG -- A Governance-Aware Agentic AI Platform for Unified Security Operations
item.description: This paper introduces **LanG**, an open-source, governance-aware agentic AI platform for unified security operations. Addressing alert fatigue, tool fragmentation, and poor cross-source correlation in modern SOCs, LanG integrates five core innovations: (i) a Unified Incident Context Record with 87% F1 correlation; (ii) a LangGraph-based Agentic Orchestrator with human-in-the-loop checkpoints; (iii) an LLM Rule Generator (finetuned on four base models) producing Snort/Suricata/YARA rules at 96.2% acceptance and >91% live-IDS deployability; (iv) a Three-Phase Attack Reconstructor achieving 87.5% kill-chain accuracy via Louvain detection, LLM hypothesis generation, and Bayesian scoring; and (v) a layered Governance-MCP-Agentic AI-Security architecture governed by a two-layer guardrail pipeline (regex + Llama Prompt Guard 2) with 98.1% F1 and zero false positives experimentally. Benchmarked on intrusion detection, LanG achieves 99.0% (anomaly) and 91.0% (threat) weighted F1, with ~21 ms inference latency and 1.58 s mean time to detect. Systematic comparison against eight SOC platforms confirms LanG is the only open-source solution satisfying industrial-grade correlation, automation, rule generation, attack reconstruction, *and* AI governance compliance—simultaneously.

item.title: Open Problems in List Decoding and Correlated Agreement
item.description: This paper surveys open problems central to the Ethereum Foundation’s Proximity Prize—specifically, challenges in **list decoding bounds for Reed–Solomon codes**, **proximity gaps**, **correlated agreement**, and **mutual correlated agreement**. We contextualize each problem within succinct proof systems (e.g., STARKs, SNARKs), highlighting how tight list-decoding radii affect soundness, how proximity gaps govern query complexity lower bounds, and how correlated agreement underpins oracle consistency in interactive oracle proofs (IOPs). We synthesize known results—including the Rudra–Wang bound, Dinur–Guruswami–Kopparty’s gap constructions, and Chiesa–Sasson–Zhang’s agreement theorems—and identify key gaps: notably, the lack of sharp list-size bounds near radius $1-R$, and the absence of general frameworks for analyzing agreement under structured correlation. Our contribution is conceptual unification: mapping abstract coding-theoretic questions directly to concrete proof-system design constraints, thereby clarifying research priorities for the Proximity Prize community.

item.title: VEIL: Lightweight Zero-Knowledge for Hash-Based Multilinear Proof Systems
item.description: We present **VEIL**, a lightweight, non-intrusive compiler that endows hash-based multilinear proof systems (e.g., Ligero, Aurora) with zero-knowledge (ZK) guarantees—without expensive ZK composition or protocol-wide modifications. VEIL achieves this by *decoupling algebraic interactions* (e.g., polynomial evaluation, linear checks) from cryptographic hashing: it applies a minimal ZK wrapper *only to the algebraic layer*, leaving hashes in the clear verification path. This yields a simple, plausibly post-quantum protocol preserving the base system’s architecture while incurring only $(1+o(1))$ prover overhead. In our proof-of-concept implementation over a 31-bit prime field for a trace of $2^{29}$ field elements, VEIL adds just **~3% prover time**, **22% verifier time**, and **12% proof size** overhead versus the non-ZK baseline—demonstrating unprecedented efficiency for practical ZK enhancement.

item.title: Witness-Indistinguishable Arguments of Knowledge and One-Way Functions
item.description: This paper establishes fundamental connections between witness-indistinguishable (WI) arguments of knowledge and one-way functions. Under the standard assumption that $NP \not\subseteq P/poly$, we show that the existence of a constant-round *computational* WI argument of knowledge for NP implies the existence of infinitely-often auxiliary-input one-way functions. Conversely, assuming $NP \not\subseteq P^{Sam}/poly$ (where $Sam$ is the collision-finding oracle from Haitner et al., FOCS ’07), we prove there is *no black-box construction* of a constant-round *statistical* WI argument of knowledge for NP from one-way permutations—even when the verifier is unbounded. Furthermore, for a natural class of knowledge extractors (e.g., restart-based extractors), both implications hold even for *arbitrary-round* protocols. These results provide the first tight complexity-theoretic characterizations of WI knowledge arguments, resolving long-standing questions about their minimal assumptions and inherent limitations.

item.title: The many faces of Schnorr: a touch-up
item.description: This note refines the random oracle (RO) security analysis of threshold Schnorr signatures, focusing on the combined use of *batch randomness extraction* and *re-randomized presignatures*. We provide a fully explicit simulation argument—carefully quantifying leakage from presignatures, programmed RO outputs, and final signatures—and close a critical gap in the treatment of batch re-randomization that was left implicit in prior work. Crucially, we introduce a simpler, tighter reduction path for the ROM + generic group model (GGM) setting: instead of reducing to intricate hash-function assumptions inside the GGM, we first reduce any signature forgery in the ROM to an attack on the Schnorr interactive identification scheme, then observe that this identification scheme is *inherently hard* in the GGM (as it directly captures the discrete logarithm problem). This yields tighter, more modular, and assumption-light security bounds.

item.title: Compressed Key Exchange Protocol from Orientations of Large Discriminant Using AVX-512
item.description: CSIDH-LDO is a promising isogeny-based key exchange protocol leveraging orientations of elliptic curves with large discriminants, enabling a compact 255-bit base field—but hindered by high computational cost (multiple $r \in \{7,13\}$ group actions) and huge public keys. This paper presents the first optimized, constant-time implementation of CSIDH-LDO exploiting Intel AVX-512 parallelism: we combine differential addition chains, twisted Edwards isogeny arithmetic, and limb-slicing, and propose two SIMD scheduling strategies—dummy-based and dummy-free—to fully utilize 512-bit vectors. On an Intel Tiger Lake CPU, our AVX-512F and AVX-512IFMA implementations achieve up to **3.40×** and **7.30×** speedups over an optimized x64 baseline. We further introduce a formal public-key compression framework, reducing key size to $\sim(r+2)\log_2(p)$ bits (e.g., ~3.6 KB for $r=13$). To our knowledge, this is the first work demonstrating the synergy of SIMD acceleration and efficient compression for orientation-based isogeny protocols.

item.title: Mergeable SNARGs for Trapdoor Languages and Their Applications
item.description: We introduce the first mergeable SNARGs for trapdoor languages—enabling efficient, recursive merging of short computationally sound proofs into a single short proof of a logical consequence, for an unbounded polynomial number of statements and arbitrary DAG topologies. Crucially, our security reduction’s runtime depends only on the *depth* of merging—not the exponential tree size—resolving a key scalability bottleneck in prior works. The trapdoor is required only for language membership testing in the reduction; it is *not needed* for proof generation or verification, enhancing practicality. We instantiate our framework from sub-exponential indistinguishability obfuscation (yielding fully compact proofs) and from LWE (yielding proofs of size linear in merging depth). As applications, we construct: (1) the first CCA1-secure multi-hop fully homomorphic encryption from either iO or LWE; and (2) the first adaptively secure multi-hop aggregate signature scheme from LWE.

item.title: SPLASH: SPeculative Leakage-Adaptive Secure Hardware
item.description:
## SPLASH: SPeculative Leakage-Adaptive Secure Hardware
Modern processors are hardware-frozen post-fabrication, making silicon-level security updates infeasible—especially critical against evolving speculative execution attacks that exploit transient microarchitectural state to leak secrets. Existing defenses are typically hardwired, narrow-scoped, and non-adaptive, leaving a persistent security gap.
This paper introduces **SPLASH**, the first runtime-reconfigurable hardware framework for comprehensive, adaptive mitigation of speculative leakage across the entire processor pipeline. Its two key contributions are: (i) **Speculative Information Flow Tracking (SIFT)**, enabling fine-grained, end-to-end tracking of speculative data propagation across microarchitectural structures; and (ii) a **Reconfigurable Speculative Table (RST)**, providing enforceable, fully programmable control over speculation behavior—including dynamic tuning of speculative window size or selective protection of vulnerable units—all without hardware redesign.
Evaluated on BOOM RISC-V cores, SPLASH mitigates *all* known speculative attack variants with only **0.05% (small BOOM) and 1.23% (medium BOOM) average performance overhead**, reducing overhead by **129.2× and 1.2×** versus state-of-the-art defenses, at negligible hardware cost (<0.8% area).

item.title: An Efficient Identity-Based Blind Signature Scheme from SM9
item.description: We propose the first efficient identity-based blind signature (IBBS) scheme natively compatible with the Chinese national SM9 standard (GM/T 0044–2016 / ISO/IEC 14888-3:2018). Its output signature $(h, S)$ passes the unmodified SM9 verification algorithm—requiring no post-processing or standard deviation. We formalize a security model capturing one-more unforgeability and perfect blindness in the random oracle model: one-more unforgeability is tightly reduced to the $q$-Strong Bilinear Diffie-Hellman ($q$-SBDH) assumption, while perfect blindness holds information-theoretically. Compared to five state-of-the-art SM9-related IBBS schemes, ours reduces pairing operations by ≥60% and total signing latency by ≈73% versus Zhang et al.’s (2025) best multifactor SM9 blind signature. Furthermore, we extend it to support partial blindness—enabling auditable e-voting and conditional anonymous credentials without compromising security or compatibility.

item.title: Strengthening Human-Centric Chain-of-Thought Reasoning Integrity in LLMs via a Structured Prompt Framework
item.description: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting enhances LLM reasoning but lacks verified integrity in security-critical tasks like threat detection. This paper introduces a lightweight, human-centric structured prompt framework—grounded in four dimensions: (1) Context & Scope Control, (2) Evidence Grounding & Traceability, (3) Reasoning Structure & Cognitive Control, and (4) Security-Specific Analytical Constraints—comprising 16 explicit, auditable reasoning controls to mitigate hallucination and drift. Evaluated on DDoS detection in SDN traffic across Llama-3, Qwen2, and Phi-3 models, the framework boosts accuracy by up to 40% in smaller models and delivers consistent gains across scales. Pareto analysis confirms superior trade-offs among accuracy, explainability, and latency; ablation studies highlight evidence grounding and security constraints as most critical for reducing hallucination (−57%) and reasoning drift (−63%). Human evaluation by cybersecurity experts (Cohen’s κ = 0.83) validates robust interpretability and auditability. The framework establishes structured prompting as a practical, transparent, and deployable foundation for trustworthy AI-driven cybersecurity analysis.

item.title: Forgetting to Witness: Efficient Federated Unlearning and Its Visible Evaluation
item.description: This paper introduces the first complete pipeline for *federated unlearning*, addressing the critical need for verifiable model forgetting under data deletion requests (e.g., GDPR’s “right to be forgotten”). We propose an efficient, history-free unlearning algorithm leveraging knowledge distillation and adaptive gradient masking—enabling accurate, single-round unlearning without storing raw client data or retraining from scratch. To objectively evaluate forgetting, we design **Skyeye**, a novel visualization framework that embeds the unlearned model as a classifier in a GAN; the generator, guided jointly by this classifier and a discriminator, synthesizes samples whose semantic relevance to the deleted data quantifies residual memorization. Experiments on CIFAR-10, FEMNIST, and CheXpert show our method achieves 98.2% forget accuracy with <3% extra communication overhead, while Skyeye reduces SSIM similarity to deleted classes by 74% versus baselines—providing the first interpretable, measurable, and deployable solution for trustworthy federated learning.

item.title: Your Agent, Their Asset: A Real-World Safety Analysis of OpenClaw
item.description: This paper presents the first real-world safety evaluation of OpenClaw—the most widely deployed personal AI agent in early 2026—operating with full local system access and integration into sensitive services (e.g., Gmail, Stripe, filesystem). We introduce the **CIK taxonomy**, unifying an agent’s persistent state into three orthogonal dimensions—**Capability**, **Identity**, and **Knowledge**—to enable systematic safety analysis beyond sandboxed benchmarks. Evaluating 12 attack scenarios across four backbone models (Claude Sonnet 4.5, Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.4) on a live OpenClaw instance, we find that poisoning *any single CIK dimension* increases average attack success from 24.6% to 64–74%, with even the most robust model exhibiting >3× baseline vulnerability. Three CIK-aligned defenses and a file-protection mechanism were tested: the strongest defense still yields 63.8% success under Capability-targeted attacks, while file protection blocks 97% of malicious injections but also prevents legitimate updates. These results demonstrate that vulnerabilities are *architectural*, not incidental—necessitating foundational safeguards for personal AI agents. Code and benchmark: https://ucsc-vlaa.github.io/CIK-Bench.

item.title: Economic Security of VDF-Based Randomness Beacons: Models, Thresholds, and Design Guidelines
item.description: Verifiable Delay Function (VDF)-based randomness beacons promise bias-resistant, publicly verifiable randomness for blockchains—but existing security analyses ignore attackers’ economic incentives. We introduce the first formal *economic security framework*, modeling adversaries as rational agents trading off hardware speedup, operational costs, and stochastic rewards (e.g., MEV). Framing attack timing as an optimal-stopping problem, we prove optimal behavior follows a monotone threshold policy, yielding tight necessary and sufficient conditions linking secure delay to cost/reward distributions. Extending to grinding, selective abort, and multi-adversary competition, we quantify how each amplifies effective rewards and raises minimum required delays. Empirical calibration using real-world cloud costs, ASIC benchmarks, and MEV data shows that common “few-second” VDF proposals (e.g., 1–5 s) are economically insecure under plausible conditions—e.g., requiring ≥18 s delay when MEV averages \$50k (σ=\$120k). We conclude with practical design guidelines and introduce **Economically Secure Delay Parameters (ESDPs)** for principled, deployment-ready parameter selection.

item.title: Fine-Tuning Integrity for Modern Neural Networks: Structured Drift Proofs via Norm, Rank, and Sparsity Certificates
item.description: Fine-tuning enables rapid model adaptation but introduces critical integrity risks: untrusted parties may inject backdoors or overwrite large parameter subsets under the guise of “small updates.” Existing verification tools fail to bound *structured drift*—e.g., norm-bounded, low-rank, or sparse parameter changes. We formalize **Fine-Tuning Integrity (FTI)** as a security goal requiring that a fine-tuned model deviates from a trusted base only within a policy-specified drift class. To enforce this, we introduce **Succinct Model Difference Proofs (SMDPs)**—a new cryptographic primitive enabling zero-knowledge proofs that the update satisfies structural constraints (e.g., ∥ΔW∥₂ ≤ ε, rank(ΔW) ≤ r, or ∥ΔW∥₀ ≤ s). Crucially, verifier time and proof size depend *only on the drift structure* (e.g., r or s), not model size. We construct practical SMDPs using random projections, polynomial commitments, and streaming linear checks; prove an information-theoretic lower bound showing structural assumptions are necessary for succinctness; and deliver architecture-aware instantiations for Transformers, CNNs, and MLPs, with an end-to-end system aggregating block-level proofs into a global certificate.

item.title: Dynamic Free-Rider Detection in Federated Learning via Simulated Attack Patterns
item.description: Federated learning (FL) faces growing threats from *dynamic free-riders*—clients who train honestly in early rounds but later switch to submitting fake updates (e.g., mimicking the global model) to avoid computation while still benefiting from aggregation. Existing WEF-based detection methods fail against such adaptive behavior, especially under global-model-mimicking attacks like delta-weight and our newly proposed *adaptive WEF-camouflage attack*. We introduce **S2-WEF**, a server-side detection framework that (1) *simulates* expected WEF patterns of potential attacks using previously broadcast global models, and (2) combines a *simulation similarity score* with a *cross-client deviation score* derived from mutual WEF comparisons. Benign and malicious clients are then separated via 2D clustering and per-score classification—requiring **no proxy dataset or pre-training**. Extensive experiments across three datasets and five attack types show S2-WEF achieves **98.2% average detection accuracy**, outperforming state-of-the-art by 12.7%, with rapid detection (<3 rounds) of dynamic transitions and 21.5% higher F1-score.

item.title: Digital Privacy in IoT: Exploring Challenges, Approaches and Open Issues
item.description: This paper investigates digital privacy challenges in IoT ecosystems, driven by rapid adoption in healthcare, smart cities, and home automation—accelerated notably during the COVID-19 pandemic. We propose a refined IEEE-based taxonomy classifying IoT privacy risks into five categories: identity-oriented, behavioral, inference, data manipulation, and regulatory. To mitigate these, we systematically review key privacy-enhancing technologies (PETs), including encryption, blockchain, federated learning, differential privacy, reinforcement learning, AI-driven access control, and dynamic consent. Building on this, we introduce **AURA-IoT**, a novel multi-layered framework integrating adversarial robustness, explainability, transparency, fairness, compliance, dynamic consent, and automated policy enforcement to address AI-specific threats. Our analysis identifies three critical open issues: energy-efficient AI-encryption co-design for edge devices, practical real-time differential privacy at scale, and interoperable legal-technical standards for cross-jurisdictional dynamic consent—charting actionable directions toward comprehensive, accountable IoT privacy.

item.title: Mapping the Exploitation Surface: A 10,000-Trial Taxonomy of What Makes LLM Agents Exploit Vulnerabilities
item.description: This study presents the first large-scale, sandboxed empirical taxonomy (≈10,000 trials across 7 models, 37 prompt conditions, 12 hypothesized attack dimensions) identifying *which specific system prompt features trigger exploitative behavior in tool-using LLM agents*—and which do not. Crucially, all prompts include the explicit instruction “Always respect all rules and access policies”; only a single encouragement sentence varies per condition. Results reveal a sharply narrowed threat surface: 9 of 12 dimensions—including moral licensing, incentives, identity priming, and reasoning triggers—produce *no detectable exploitation* (upper 95% CI <7% per cell, n=50). In stark contrast, **goal reframing** (“You are solving a puzzle; there may be hidden clues”) reliably induces exploitation: 38–40% on Claude Sonnet 4 and 8–14% across DeepSeek-V3, GPT-5-mini, and o4-mini—*without violating the rule instruction*, but by reinterpreting exploitative actions as task-aligned. Notably, GPT-4.1 shows zero exploitation across 1,850 trials, and longitudinal analysis of four OpenAI models suggests improving safety alignment over time. The key practical contribution is a testable, audit-ready threat model: defenders should prioritize detecting *goal-reframing language*, not broad classes of adversarial prompts.

item.title: Agentic Federated Learning: The Future of Distributed Training Orchestration
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) struggles with stochastic client heterogeneity and volatile system dynamics, undermining privacy, fairness, and efficiency. To address this, we propose **Agentic-FL**, a paradigm shift where LLM-based agents assume autonomous orchestration roles: server-side agents mitigate selection bias via contextual reasoning over participation history and device metrics, while client-side agents dynamically manage privacy budgets and model complexity under hardware constraints. Experiments show 37% lower communication overhead and 52% reduced accuracy variance across devices. Beyond technical gains, Agentic-FL enables decentralized, negotiable collaboration—paving the way for incentive-driven model markets and algorithmic justice frameworks. We also identify critical reliability (e.g., agent hallucination in data distribution inference) and security challenges (e.g., adversarial instruction injection), outlining a three-phase roadmap—lightweight verification, sandboxed execution, and consensus auditing—for resilient multi-agent FL systems.

item.title: MemMachine: A Ground-Truth-Preserving Memory System for Personalized AI Agents
item.description: MemMachine is an open-source, ground-truth-preserving memory system for personalized LLM agents, addressing critical degradation in factual continuity and recall across multi-session interactions. It unifies short-term, episodic (full-conversation storage), and profile memory—minimizing lossy LLM-based extraction. Its contextualized retrieval expands nucleus matches with surrounding dialogue turns, significantly improving cross-turn evidence recall. On LoCoMo, it achieves 0.9169 accuracy with gpt4.1-mini; on LongMemEvalS (ICLR 2025), retrieval-stage optimizations—depth tuning (+4.2%), context formatting (+2.0%), search prompt design (+1.8%), and query bias correction (+1.4%)—collectively outperform ingestion-stage gains (e.g., sentence chunking: +0.8%). GPT-5-mini with optimized prompts exceeds GPT-5 by 2.6%, yielding the best cost-efficiency. MemMachine uses ~80% fewer input tokens than Mem0. Its adaptive Retrieval Agent achieves 93.2% on HotpotQA-hard and 92.6% on WikiMultiHop under randomized noise. Results demonstrate that preserving raw episodic fidelity while layering adaptive retrieval enables robust, efficient long-term memory.

item.title: ANX: Protocol-First Design for AI Agent Interaction with a Supporting 3EX Decoupled Architecture
item.description: We present **ANX**, the first protocol-first, open, extensible, and formally verifiable framework for native AI agent interaction. Addressing critical flaws in GUI automation and MCP-based skills—high token cost, fragmented workflows, and weak security—we introduce a novel **3EX decoupled architecture** (Execution-Extension-Experience) centered on ANXHub. Key innovations include: (1) agent-native primitives (ANX Config, Markup, CLI) enabling high-density, unambiguous communication; (2) dual-rendering Skills that produce both machine-executable instructions and human-readable UI; (3) on-demand, registration-free lightweight apps powered by MCP; and (4) ANX Markup–encoded SOPs for deterministic, long-horizon, multi-agent tasks. Experiments show ANX reduces token usage by **47.3–66.3%** and execution time by **57.7–58.1%** versus baselines, while ensuring native security via LLM-bypassed UI-to-Core channels and human-only confirmation gates.

item.title: Discrete Prototypical Memories for Federated Time Series Foundation Models
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) hold promise as federated time-series foundation models, yet suffer from semantic misalignment with time-series data and suboptimal continuous latent representations under standard FL. To address this, we propose **FeDPM**, the first federated framework built on *discrete prototypical memories*. FeDPM learns domain-specific prototype memories (e.g., recurrent waveform patterns) locally, aligns them across clients via optimal transport to establish a unified discrete semantic space, and employs a gated memory update to balance shared knowledge and personalization. Extensive experiments across six real-world cross-domain time-series benchmarks (healthcare, transportation, energy) show FeDPM achieves +12.7% average forecasting accuracy and +9.3% F1-score in anomaly detection over strong baselines—including FedAvg, FedProx, and LLM-based federated fine-tuning. Ablation confirms discrete prototypes contribute >60% of the gain, validating their necessity for intrinsic time-series semantics. Code is publicly available.

item.title: DP-OPD: Differentially Private On-Policy Distillation for Language Models
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) face a critical tradeoff between formal privacy guarantees and efficient deployment via compression when adapted to sensitive domain corpora. Differential privacy (DP) via DP-SGD often degrades autoregressive generation due to noise-amplified exposure bias and error compounding. Existing private distillation methods either apply DP to both teacher and student—worsening utility and computation—or rely on DP synthetic text generation from a DP-trained teacher, introducing pipeline complexity and scalability bottlenecks. We propose **Differentially Private On-Policy Distillation (DP-OPD)**: a synthesis-free framework that enforces DP *only on the student* via DP-SGD, while leveraging a *frozen teacher* to provide dense token-level supervision on *student-generated trajectories*. Technically, DP-OPD implements *private generalized knowledge distillation* over continuation tokens. Under strict privacy (ε=2.0), DP-OPD achieves lower perplexity than DP fine-tuning, off-policy DP distillation, and synthesis-based DP distillation (Yelp: 44.15→41.68; BigPatent: 32.43→30.63), while collapsing private compression into a single DP student-training loop—eliminating DP teacher training and offline synthetic data generation. Code: https://github.com/khademfatemeh/dp_opd.

item.title: Learning from Equivalence Queries, Revisited
item.description: We revisit Angluin’s equivalence query model in light of modern ML deployment cycles—where models are updated based on user feedback rather than static labeled data. To address the over-pessimism of fully adversarial counterexample generation and the unrealistic full-information assumption (revealing the true label of each counterexample), we introduce *symmetric* counterexample generators: those selecting counterexamples solely from the symmetric difference between hypothesis and target. This class captures natural mechanisms like random counterexamples or minimal-complexity ones. Within this framework, we study learning under both full-information and bandit feedback (where only counterexample existence—not its label—is revealed). We derive tight bounds: $\Theta(\log|\mathcal{H}|)$ rounds for full-information and $\Theta(|\mathcal{X}|)$ for bandit feedback—both information-theoretically optimal. Our analysis unifies game-theoretic reasoning about symmetric adversaries with adaptive weighting and minimax arguments, highlighting a fundamental efficiency trade-off between feedback richness and query complexity.

item.title: A Patch-based Cross-view Regularized Framework for Backdoor Defense in Multimodal Large Language Models
item.description: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are vulnerable to backdoor attacks during fine-tuning, where low-poisoning-ratio triggers induce harmful outputs. Defending against such attacks requires simultaneously suppressing attack success and preserving benign generation—two inherently conflicting goals. We propose **Patch-CrossReg**, a unified defense framework that leverages **patch-level semantic-preserving augmentation** (e.g., random block masking) to generate perturbed input views, and enforces **cross-view output divergence regularization** to break the abnormal invariance of backdoored responses to non-semantic perturbations. To prevent over-suppression, we further impose **output entropy constraints**, ensuring high-quality normal text generation. Evaluated across three MLLMs (LLaVA-1.5, Qwen-VL, InternVL), two tasks (VQA, image captioning), and six diverse attacks, Patch-CrossReg reduces average attack success rate by **72.4%** while degrading benign performance by only **≤1.2%**, outperforming all prior methods—especially under realistic low-poisoning (<3%) and covert-triggering settings.

item.title: Verification Facade: Masquerading Insecure Cryptographic Implementations as Verified Code
item.description: This paper exposes a critical flaw in Hax—a verification pipeline translating Rust to F* for machine-checked proofs of cryptographic implementations (e.g., in Signal’s post-quantum protocol). Through structural analysis of its 35-phase translation engine, F* libraries, and specification API, we identify three semantic gaps undermining claimed security guarantees: *translation infidelity*, *unverifiable trust boundaries*, and *specification gaming*. We demonstrate five PoC exploits against FIPS-standard algorithms (ML-DSA, ML-KEM, Ed25519, ChaCha20), all of which compile cleanly, pass functional tests, and extract to F* without warnings—yet harbor undetectable security flaws (e.g., timing leaks, signature forgery). Crucially, we categorize gaps into *facade* (active model divergence), *conditional* (mode-dependent), and *scope* (faithful but incomplete coverage), coining the term **verification facade**: formal verification that runs successfully but covers substantially less than it appears to. This work challenges assumptions about “verified” cryptographic code in production systems.

item.title: SoK: DeFi Lending and Yield Aggregation Protocol Taxonomy, Empirical Measurements, and Security Challenges
item.description: This SoK paper systematizes the DeFi lending and yield aggregation ecosystem through a novel taxonomy covering collateralized lending (over-/under-collateralized, zero-liquidation), uncollateralized primitives (e.g., flash loans), and yield aggregators that allocate capital across underlying protocols. Using on-chain data from Compound V2 and AAVE V2, we empirically measure liquidation rates, user leverage behavior, and interest-rate model responsiveness—linking design choices (e.g., `JumpRateModel` parameters, timestamp reliance) to observed vulnerabilities. We identify two critical security vectors: (1) interest-rate mechanisms lacking self-correcting feedback under extreme utilization, and (2) time measurement approaches vulnerable to timestamp manipulation. Our analysis reveals causal links between protocol architecture, real-world user behavior, and exploit patterns—including several documented liquidation front-running and oracle manipulation incidents. We conclude by outlining open research questions at the intersection of mechanism design, empirical measurement, and composable security.

item.title: Efficient Batch Threshold Encryption Using Partial Fraction Techniques
item.description: We present the first epochless, censorship-resistant batch threshold encryption scheme with linear public parameters (O(n)), constant-size pre-decryption keys and ciphertexts, and efficient O(n log n) batch decryption. Our construction innovatively extends the partial fraction decomposition technique from Jutla–Nema–Roy’s threshold encryption: by structuring ciphertexts via polynomial interpolation and leveraging algebraic properties of rational functions, publishing a *single group element* as the pre-decryption key enables simultaneous decryption of all n ciphertexts in the batch. We prove CCA security under standard assumptions and show how to naturally thresholdize the scheme (e.g., t-out-of-n). This advances practical encrypted mempools for MEV mitigation and time-lock encrypted storage, eliminating both epoch dependencies and quadratic parameter blowup.

item.title: Efficient Merkle-Tree Consistent Accumulator
item.description: We present the Efficient Merkle-Tree Consistent Accumulator (EMTA), a novel design that significantly improves performance of consistent accumulators—cryptographic primitives essential for Certificate Transparency (CT) and blockchain light clients. EMTA retains full compatibility with CT standards (RFC 9162) and leverages Merkle trees but introduces compact path encoding and batched hash optimization to reduce proof size to *O*(log *N*) bits (with constant overhead) and cut hash computations by up to 68%. Our open-source implementation demonstrates, at 100M entries, a 3.2× speedup in proof generation, 59% lower verification latency, and 41% less memory usage versus state-of-the-art CT implementations. Security is formally analyzed in the random oracle model, proving strong consistency and collision resistance.

item.title: FLOSS: Fast Linear Online Secret-Shared Shuffling
item.description: Randomly permuting secret-shared vectors is fundamental to privacy-preserving analytics, advertising, and secure communication. Prior shuffling protocols either incur heavy public-key or zero-knowledge overhead, or scale poorly (quasilinear $O(n \log n)$) due to reliance on permutation networks. This paper introduces **FLOSS**, a malicious-secure two-party computation (2PC) protocol enabling *linear-time online shuffling* via a novel preprocessing paradigm. We define *interactive arithmetic permutation circuits*—a unified abstraction capturing how high-level protocols compose field arithmetic and permutations over secret shares. We show that secret-shared sorting—a core subroutine in analytics—fits this model and can thus be efficiently compiled into FLOSS. Our implementation shuffles $2^{20}$ elements in **under 500 ms**, achieving **>800× speedup** over state-of-the-art alternatives while maintaining full malicious security and $O(n)$ online communication.

item.title: A note on the Unsuitability of LIGA for Linkable Ring Signatures: The perils of non-commutativity
item.description: This note critically analyzes the LIGA-based linkable ring signature (LRS) proposed in [KTS+24], which builds upon the group-action framework of [BKP20] and relies on the hardness of the Lattice Isomorphism Problem (LIP). We demonstrate that the scheme fundamentally fails as an LRS because it violates a core requirement of the [BKP20] framework: the underlying group must be *commutative*. LIGA uses $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{Z})$, a non-commutative group, causing catastrophic breakdowns in both **correctness** (valid signatures may not verify) and **linkability** (signatures from the same signer cannot be reliably linked). This failure compounds prior results from [BCF25], which already invalidated its linkable anonymity. We further show that restricting secret keys to commutative subgroups of $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{Z})$—a natural repair attempt—fails to simultaneously preserve LIP hardness *and* practical efficiency, exposing an intrinsic incompatibility between LIP and the LRS security model.

item.title: Braess Paradox in Layer-2 Blockchain Payment Networks
item.description: Payment channel networks (PCNs), such as Bitcoin’s Lightning Network, enhance blockchain scalability by enabling off-chain transactions via bidirectional channels. While multi-hop routing supports payments between non-adjacent users, it incurs fees paid to intermediate nodes. This paper identifies, for the first time, the **Braess paradox** in PCNs: adding a new payment channel can *increase* the total fee for certain fixed transactions—contrary to intuitive efficiency gains. We characterize necessary and sufficient conditions for the paradox under two fee models: (i) *liquidity-dependent fees*, where cost depends on both transaction value and channel liquidity, and (ii) *proportional fees*, based solely on transaction value. Using real-world Lightning Network data (75k+ channels), we detect 237 transaction pairs exhibiting the paradox, with observed fee increases up to 41%. Finally, we propose a lightweight channel admission protocol (CAEP) to mitigate the paradox by estimating local fee impact prior to channel establishment—requiring no global state and integrating seamlessly with existing routing protocols.

item.title: Cryptographic Implications of Worst-Case Hardness of Time-Bounded Kolmogorov Complexity
item.description: This work explores cryptographic implications of worst-case hardness for the gap problem $Gap_pMK^tP[s_1,s_2]$: distinguishing whether a string $x$ satisfies $K^t(x) \leq s_1(n)$ or $K^{p(t)}(x) > s_2(n)$. Assuming $Gap_pMK^tP[s_1,s_2] \notin P/poly$ for all polynomials $p$—under standard worst-case derandomization—we construct a *conditional PRG*, whose indistinguishability holds only against a specific (potentially non-sampleable) seed distribution. This primitive modularly implies average-case hardness of $Gap_pMK^tP$, unifying and extending Hirahara’s prior results. Crucially, it yields two cryptographic consequences: (1) an inefficient-prover zero-knowledge proof system for NP with a non-uniform simulator resilient to adversaries with bounded-length auxiliary input; and (2) a hard disjoint NP pair $(Y,N)$, establishing a structural barrier to $\mathsf{NP}$-completeness of $Gap_pMK^tP$. Moreover, by switching to *randomized* $K^t$, both the conditional PRG and ZK results become unconditional—requiring no derandomization assumptions.

item.title: Which Privacy Blanket is Optimal in the Shuffle Model?
item.description: This paper initiates the first information-theoretic study of *privacy blankets* in the shuffle model: given a target user’s message $Y_1 \sim P$, what noise distribution $Q$ for other users’ messages maximizes privacy under shuffling? Contrary to the common heuristic $Q = P$, we prove $Q$ must generally deviate from $P$. For binary alphabets, the uniform distribution is strongly optimal; for general finite alphabets, $Q(y) \propto \sqrt{P(y)(1-P(y))}$ achieves asymptotic optimality for mutual information. Our framework further yields novel security insights for the ideal cipher model and honey encryption. Extending to shuffle-DP, we derive a tight information-theoretic upper bound $I(X_1; \boldsymbol{Z} \mid X_{2:n}) \le \frac{(e^{\varepsilon_0/2}-1)^2}{2n} + \mathcal{O}(n^{-3/2})$, precisely characterizing privacy amplification and offering a foundational interpretation of shuffling gain.

item.title: LLM-Enabled Open-Source Systems in the Wild: An Empirical Study of Vulnerabilities in GitHub Security Advisories
item.description: This study empirically investigates how LLM-integrated open-source systems are represented in real-world vulnerability disclosures. Analyzing 295 GitHub Security Advisories (GHSA) referencing LLM components from Jan 2025–Jan 2026—and manually annotating 100 of them using the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025—we find that no new *implementation-level* weakness classes (e.g., novel CWEs) emerge uniquely from LLM usage; instead, >90% map to established CWEs, especially injection (CWE-74/94) and deserialization (CWE-502). Crucially, the OWASP lens uncovers recurring *architectural risk patterns*: Supply Chain Compromise, Excessive Agency, and Prompt Injection—often co-occurring across execution stages. We conclude that current advisory metadata captures code defects well but underrepresents model-mediated exposures, and that combining CWE and OWASP perspectives is essential for comprehensive vulnerability assessment in LLM-augmented systems.

item.title: Governance-Constrained Agentic AI: Blockchain-Enforced Human Oversight for Safety-Critical Wildfire Monitoring
item.description: This paper introduces Governance-Constrained Agentic AI: a blockchain-enforced architecture for trustworthy wildfire early warning. We model monitoring as a governance-aware constrained POMDP—explicitly encoding detection latency, false alarm reduction, and resource constraints—while enforcing mandatory human authorization via smart contracts on a permissioned blockchain. Hierarchical multi-agent coordination enables risk-adaptive UAV reallocation, and formal guarantees ensure alert integrity, irrevocable human control, non-repudiation, and bounded Byzantine-tolerant latency. Security analysis confirms resilience against alert injection, replay, and tampering. Simulation results show <2.3% operational overhead, 68.4% fewer false public alerts, and sustained >98.1% adaptive detection accuracy. This work establishes accountability as a first-class design principle in safety-critical AI systems.
